text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Medical Oncology — Melanoma Program

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
AGE: 58 years
DIAGNOSIS: Stage IV (AJCC 8th ed., M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, arising from the back
LINE OF THERAPY: First-line — Dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg PO BID + Trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg PO QD
DURATION ON CURRENT REGIMEN: Approximately 8 months
ECOG PERFORMANCE STATUS: 1–2

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu presents today for scheduled restaging evaluation and disease management review. She reports new onset of left axillary pain over the past 3–4 weeks, increased fatigue compared to her prior nadir on therapy, and recurrence of mild right upper quadrant (RUQ) discomfort. She also notes a new palpable firmness in the right groin, which she first noticed approximately 2 weeks ago and found concerning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old woman with a history of dysplastic nevus syndrome who was diagnosed with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV cutaneous melanoma arising from the trunk approximately 9–10 months ago. Her disease at diagnosis was notable for bulky left axillary lymphadenopathy (4.8 cm with central necrosis), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm in segment VI), and subcutaneous satellite nodules along the left flank. She had an elevated LDH of 412 U/L at diagnosis. Comprehensive molecular profiling by a 500-gene solid tumor NGS panel revealed BRAF p.V600E (VAF 48%), co-occurring NF1 p.Q1300* loss-of-function mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, TERT promoter C228T, and TP53 p.R248W, with a high tumor mutational burden of 42 mutations/Mb and PD-L1 TPS 30%. Brain MRI at staging was negative for intracranial disease.

Given the BRAF V600E mutation, bulky hepatic disease, significantly elevated LDH, and clinical urgency for disease control, she was initiated on first-line dabrafenib/trametinib approximately 3 weeks after completing staging. Her early course was complicated by grade 1 pyrexia (Tmax 38.2°C), fatigue, and nausea approximately 3 weeks into treatment, managed with a brief 3-day dabrafenib hold followed by restart at full dose without formal dose reduction — toxicities resolved within one week.

At her 8-week restaging CT, she demonstrated a dramatic response: the left axillary node shrank from 4.8 cm to 1.9 cm, the dominant hepatic lesion decreased from 3.2 cm to 1.1 cm, and subcutaneous nodules regressed from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm. LDH had normalized to 155 U/L. She reported feeling substantially better, with resolution of RUQ discomfort and recovery of lost weight. Treatment was continued at full doses.

At approximately 5 months into therapy, she developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (PPE) affecting bilateral palms and soles with associated hyperkeratosis. Trametinib was dose-reduced to 1.5 mg QD with intensive urea cream and emollient use. Symptoms improved to grade 1 within 3 weeks and trametinib was successfully escalated back to 2 mg QD.

At the 6-month restaging CT, disease remained stable and in partial response (left axillary node 1.8 cm, dominant hepatic lesion 1.0 cm, subcutaneous nodule 0.4 cm), with LDH continuing within normal limits at 160 U/L.

The patient now returns approximately 8 months after treatment initiation with the above new symptoms. Today's restaging CT (reviewed in preparation for this visit) demonstrates unequivocal disease progression: the left axillary node has enlarged from 1.8 cm to 3.4 cm with new central necrosis, the dominant hepatic segment VI lesion has grown from 1.0 cm to 2.6 cm, two new hepatic lesions are present in segments II (1.5 cm) and IVa (1.2 cm), the left flank subcutaneous nodule has re-enlarged to 1.4 cm, and there is a new right inguinal lymph node measuring 1.2 cm — a site not previously involved. LDH has re-elevated to 285 U/L. Brain MRI has been ordered and is pending at the time of this visit (results to be reviewed upon availability).

These findings are entirely consistent with acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy, which is well-recognized after a median of approximately 6–12 months on BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations. Given the genomic context — particularly co-occurring NF1 loss-of-function, MAP2K1 amplification, and high TMB — multiple potential resistance mechanisms may be operative, including RAS-pathway reactivation and MEK bypass signaling. The clinical picture is one of classic acquired resistance.

The patient has been counseled at length today regarding the findings and the rationale for transitioning therapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports increased fatigue over the past 3–4 weeks; estimates her energy at approximately 60–70% of her best nadir during treatment. Intermittent low-grade fevers (Tmax 37.8°C at home). Weight has been stable since her recovery from early treatment toxicity; she denies further weight loss over the past month but notes decreased appetite.
Pain: New aching discomfort in the left axilla and upper arm, rated 3–4/10, non-radiating. Recurrent right upper quadrant discomfort, described as a dull heaviness, similar in character to her pre-treatment symptoms. Denies bone pain.
Gastrointestinal: Mild decrease in appetite. No nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. No diarrhea, hematochezia, or melena. Last bowel movement was yesterday, formed and brown.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or frequency changes.
Neurological: Denies headache, visual changes, dizziness, focal weakness, or numbness. No new neurological symptoms.
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Skin/Integument: Bilateral palms and soles with mild residual dryness from prior PPE; no active blistering or significant pain. Denies rash elsewhere. She has not noted any new suspicious skin lesions on self-examination, though notes she has been less vigilant given her systemic illness concerns.
Endocrine/Other: No excessive thirst or urination. Denies palpitations. No cold or heat intolerance.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (reviewed and updated)
- Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma (primary: T4b, ulcerated, Breslow 4.2 mm, Clark level V, trunk) — as above
- Dysplastic nevus syndrome with multiple prior wide-local excisions of atypical melanocytic nevi; last Derm surveillance was approximately 12 months ago
- No prior history of autoimmune disease
- No prior history of inflammatory bowel disease
- No significant cardiovascular, pulmonary, or renal history

SOCIAL HISTORY
- High school science teacher; currently on reduced-schedule medical leave since melanoma diagnosis
- Never-smoker
- Alcohol: social, approximately 1–2 drinks per week
- Lives with her adult daughter; good social support
- West African and British descent
- No illicit drug use

FAMILY HISTORY (reviewed)
- Maternal uncle: cutaneous melanoma (age of diagnosis not known)
- No other known family history of melanoma or other malignancies
- No known family history of autoimmune disease

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies
- No known food or environmental allergies

MEDICATIONS (current, prior to today's plan change)
1. Dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg PO BID — BRAF inhibitor; first-line targeted therapy
2. Trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg PO QD — MEK inhibitor; first-line targeted therapy
3. Urea 40% cream — applied to bilateral palms/soles BID for PPE management (PRN)
4. Emollient/moisturizing lotion — heavy cream applied QD–BID to bilateral extremities
5. Ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN — for nausea (infrequent use in recent months)
6. Acetaminophen 500–650 mg PO PRN — for fever/mild pain, per standing guidance

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Temperature 37.4°C (oral); Blood Pressure 122/76 mmHg; Heart Rate 84 bpm (regular); Respiratory Rate 16 breaths/min; SpO2 98% on room air; Weight 64.2 kg (up from nadir of ~61 kg during initial presentation; baseline pre-illness approximately 66 kg).

General: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a well-appearing woman who looks her stated age. She is alert and oriented, in no acute distress at rest. She appears mildly fatigued compared to her 8-week visit.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae not pale. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis, no oral lesions.

Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Chest and Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. No dullness to percussion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness on deep palpation in the right upper quadrant; no involuntary guarding or rebound tenderness. Liver edge palpable approximately 1–2 cm below the right costal margin. No splenomegaly. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.

Lymph Nodes:
- Left axilla: A firm, mildly tender lymph node mass measuring approximately 3.5 cm is palpable, somewhat fixed to surrounding tissues. This represents clear interval enlargement compared to the prior exam (~2 cm, soft, barely palpable at the 8-week visit).
- Right inguinal region: A new, approximately 1.0–1.2 cm firm, non-tender lymph node is palpable in the right inguinal crease, consistent with imaging findings of a new inguinal node.
- Bilateral cervical, supraclavicular, submandibular, and contralateral inguinal regions: No palpable lymphadenopathy.

Skin: Multiple surgical scars on the back and flanks consistent with prior wide-local excisions of atypical nevi. Along the left flank, the previously regressing subcutaneous nodule is again palpable at approximately 1.2–1.5 cm — firm, non-tender, mobile. Mild dryness and hyperpigmentation of bilateral palms and soles, consistent with resolving PPE (grade 1 residual). No new suspicious pigmented lesions appreciated on gross skin survey (though a formal full-body skin examination by dermatology is warranted).

Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities bilaterally. Sensation grossly intact. Gait steady.

Musculoskeletal: No point tenderness over vertebral column or long bones. Full range of motion in upper and lower extremities without pain.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (from today's draw)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 7,200/mm³ (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 22%, monocytes 8%, eosinophils 1%, basophils 1%)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, consistent with inflammatory/chronic disease anemia; at prior visit Hgb was 12.4 g/dL)
- Platelets: 228,000/mm³

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L | Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L | Chloride: 102 mEq/L | CO2: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL | Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL | eGFR: >60 mL/min/1.73m²
- Glucose: 92 mg/dL
- Calcium: 9.3 mg/dL
- Total Protein: 7.1 g/dL | Albumin: 3.7 g/dL
- Total Bilirubin: 1.1 mg/dL | Direct Bilirubin: 0.3 mg/dL
- AST: 55 U/L (elevated; ULN ~35 U/L) | ALT: 44 U/L (mildly elevated; ULN ~40 U/L)
- Alkaline Phosphatase: 168 U/L (mildly elevated; ULN ~120 U/L)

Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH): 285 U/L (ULN 171 U/L — approximately 1.7x ULN; up from 160 U/L at 6-month restaging and 155 U/L at 8-week restaging)

Thyroid Function: TSH 2.8 mIU/L (within normal limits — not felt to be immunotherapy-related given she has not yet received checkpoint inhibitors; will recheck at subsequent visit)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.0, PTT 29 seconds — within normal limits.

Inflammatory markers: CRP 18.4 mg/L (mildly elevated; reflects active systemic tumor burden)

Urinalysis: Clear, no hematuria, no proteinuria.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed prior to today's visit)

CT Chest, Abdomen, and Pelvis with Contrast (restaging, obtained 2 days prior to this visit):
This study was reviewed in detail. The key findings are as follows: The left axillary lymph node, which had shrunk dramatically with initial dabrafenib/trametinib, has re-enlarged substantially — now measuring 3.4 cm (up from 1.8 cm on the 6-month scan) with new central necrosis. In the liver, the dominant segment VI lesion has grown from 1.0 cm back to 2.6 cm; two entirely new hepatic lesions are now present in segment II (1.5 cm) and segment IVa (1.2 cm). The subcutaneous nodule on the left flank is back to 1.4 cm (from a nadir of ~0.4 cm). Most concerning, a new right inguinal lymph node measuring 1.2 cm has appeared — a site not previously involved — indicating spread to a new nodal basin. There are no pulmonary nodules and no bony lesions. The spleen and pancreas appear normal.

Taken together, this represents unambiguous disease progression with growth of all previously responding lesions, the appearance of two new hepatic deposits, and emergence of a new inguinal metastasis. This is fully consistent with the clinical picture of acquired BRAF/MEK inhibitor resistance.

Brain MRI with and without gadolinium (ordered at today's visit, result pending): This will be reviewed promptly upon availability. Given the absence of neurological symptoms and the pattern of acquired resistance predominantly in visceral and nodal sites, the likelihood of symptomatic CNS progression is considered lower, but imaging is critical to rule out brain metastases before initiating combination immunotherapy.

---

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old woman with BRAF V600E-mutant, NF1-comutated Stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma who initially responded very well to first-line dabrafenib/trametinib — achieving a near-50–60% reduction in disease burden as early as 8 weeks into therapy, with LDH normalization and significant symptomatic improvement. She now presents at approximately 8 months with clear radiographic and clinical evidence of acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition. This is expected given that the median duration of response to this class of agents in metastatic melanoma is typically in the range of 9–12 months, and her molecular profile (NF1 loss, MAP2K1 amplification, high mutational burden) suggests a biologically complex tumor with multiple potential bypass mechanisms.

The re-elevation of LDH to 285 U/L (approximately 1.7x ULN), the return of hepatic and nodal disease, the appearance of two new hepatic lesions, and the involvement of a new inguinal nodal station collectively indicate that the disease is actively progressing and that continued dabrafenib/trametinib will not provide meaningful benefit. There are no features of pseudoprogression in this context — targeted therapy does not produce delayed responses.

She has a highly actionable immunologic profile: PD-L1 TPS 30%, and a very high TMB of 42 mutations/Mb. She did not receive upfront immunotherapy (a reasonable decision given the urgent visceral disease control need at diagnosis). Her performance status remains ECOG 1–2, she has no active autoimmune disease, no prior immune-related adverse events, and is otherwise a good candidate for dual checkpoint blockade with ipilimumab and nivolumab. This combination (ipi 3 mg/kg + nivo 1 mg/kg every 3 weeks x 4 cycles, then nivo maintenance) represents the standard second-line approach in this setting and offers the potential for durable responses.

Key concerns prior to initiating checkpoint immunotherapy:
1. Brain MRI result — must confirm absence of symptomatic or large brain metastases before proceeding with ipi/nivo; if small asymptomatic brain metastases are identified, combination immunotherapy remains an option but would merit neurosurgery or radiation oncology co-evaluation.
2. Liver function — current mild transaminase and alkaline phosphatase elevations are most likely attributable to progressive hepatic metastases rather than drug-induced liver injury (as she is being transitioned off dabrafenib/trametinib). These should be monitored closely, as hepatic immune-related adverse events can complicate ipi/nivo therapy.
3. Bowel history — she has no prior history of inflammatory bowel disease. She should be counseled that immune-mediated colitis is the most common serious adverse event with ipi/nivo, occurring in 15–25% of patients, and that prompt reporting of diarrhea is imperative.

---

PLAN

1. Disease Progression on Dabrafenib/Trametinib — Transition to Second-Line Immunotherapy:
   - Dabrafenib and trametinib to be DISCONTINUED today. Patient instructed to stop both medications after today.
   - Plan to initiate combination ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV + nivolumab (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles (approximately 12 weeks), followed by nivolumab monotherapy maintenance at 480 mg IV every 4 weeks.
   - Rationale: PD-L1 30%, high TMB (42 mut/Mb), and patient fitness support dual checkpoint blockade. The ipi/nivo combination offers the highest response rate in this setting, with a proportion of patients achieving durable long-term disease control.
   - Prior authorization and insurance pre-certification to be initiated by nursing/pharmacy team today.
   - Pre-treatment labs for immunotherapy clearance: CBC, CMP, LFTs, LDH, TSH, fasting glucose, HbA1c — to be obtained within 1 week prior to cycle 1.
   - Cycle 1 targeted for approximately 1–2 weeks from today, pending brain MRI result and lab clearance.

2. Brain MRI (ordered today):
   - Result to be reviewed within 24–48 hours. Patient will be contacted directly with results.
   - If brain MRI is clear: proceed with immunotherapy as planned.
   - If brain metastases identified: will discuss urgently with neurosurgery and/or radiation oncology as appropriate before initiating systemic therapy.

3. Toxicity Counseling — Immune-Related Adverse Events (irAEs):
   - Extensive patient education performed today regarding the spectrum of irAEs associated with ipi/nivo, with particular emphasis on:
     a. Colitis/diarrhea: Instruct to call if > 4 bowel movements above baseline per day, or any bloody/mucous stools — do NOT wait for next scheduled visit.
     b. Hepatitis: Liver function will be checked before each cycle.
     c. Pneumonitis: New or worsening cough, dyspnea, or O2 requirement — immediate evaluation.
     d. Endocrinopathies: Fatigue, headache, visual changes (pituitary), polyuria/polydipsia (diabetes insipidus or type 1 DM), new cold intolerance (thyroid).
     e. Dermatitis: Rash, pruritus — report grade 2 or higher.
     f. Neurotoxicity: Any new focal neurological symptoms.
   - Written irAE management card provided. 24-hour oncology phone number reviewed.
   - Patient expressed understanding and appropriate concern; verbalized willingness to call with any symptoms.

4. Labs (today):
   - CBC, CMP, LFTs, LDH, CRP, TSH reviewed above. Mild anemia and LFT elevation noted — attributed to disease progression. Will repeat in 2 weeks to ensure no ongoing deterioration prior to cycle 1.

5. Hepatic Lesions and LFT Monitoring:
   - Given presence of hepatic metastases and mildly elevated LFTs, a hepatology consult is not indicated at this time, but LFTs will be closely monitored pre-cycle and mid-cycle.
   - If LFTs worsen substantially before immunotherapy initiation, reassessment and potential hepatology involvement will occur.

6. Supportive Care:
   - Acetaminophen PRN for axillary pain (avoid NSAIDs given hepatic involvement and potential immunotherapy interactions).
   - Nutritional counseling referral placed given mild decrease in appetite and anemia.
   - Social work referral renewed — patient is on reduced-schedule work status and may need additional support during a new and more intense treatment phase.

7. Dermatology Follow-up:
   - Recommend dermatology follow-up for whole-body skin examination given dysplastic nevus syndrome history; any new or changing lesions on the back/trunk require prompt evaluation.

8. Next Oncology Appointment:
   - Will be seen in clinic on day 1 of cycle 1 of ipilimumab/nivolumab (approximately 1–2 weeks from today).
   - Subsequent visits will be every 3 weeks for the duration of induction (cycles 1–4), then every 4 weeks on nivolumab maintenance.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned after 4 cycles of induction (approximately 12 weeks from first immunotherapy dose).

9. Brain MRI Results Follow-Up:
   - Nurse coordinator to contact patient within 48 hours with MRI result.
   - If negative: confirm cycle 1 scheduling.
   - If positive: urgent multidisciplinary discussion.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes, including detailed discussion of imaging findings, change of therapy rationale, and irAE counseling. All questions answered. Patient verbalized understanding and consent for the proposed treatment transition.

Electronically signed,
Attending Oncologist, Melanoma Program
Medical Oncology
